mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned SLS AMG engine typically costs £8,500–£12,000 supply only. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800 depending on the specialist. Customers can save up to £3,500 versus main dealer pricing on common repairs. A full engine replacement can easily exceed £15,000 with labour. Rebuilt engines often include upgraded M159 oil pump gears to prevent common seizure. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your SLS.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M159 is a handcrafted masterpiece derived from the M156 with significant improvements, including dry‑sump lubrication, new camshafts, headers, and improved valve buckets. However, the SLS demands careful ownership. Common issues include oil starvation during aggressive cornering (addressed by AMG Technical Bulletin TSB-AMG-11-03), camshaft phaser bolt failure, oil leaks from valve cover gaskets, and crankcase breather valve failure. With proper maintenance and proactive upgrades (dry‑sump scavenge pumps, ARP phaser bolts), the M159 can be a reliable engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are oil starvation during aggressive cornering (bearings can fail if oil control is inadequate — addressed by upgraded dry‑sump scavenge pumps per AMG TSB-AMG-11-03), camshaft phaser bolt failure (factory torque‑to‑yield bolts can weaken — upgrade to ARP bolts), oil leaks from valve cover gaskets and camshaft solenoid covers, crankcase breather valve failure (diaphragm deterioration — replace at 60,000 miles), and DCT transmission jerkiness at low speeds.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an SLS AMG engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and condition. Against a well‑kept SLS AMG worth £80,000–£200,000+, a £10,000–£12,000 recon is 5–15% of the car's value — worth it. The SLS is a future classic and engine replacement is a justifiable investment for a cherished example. GT and Black Series models command the highest premiums.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is M159.980, found on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation. It's the 6.2‑litre naturally aspirated V8 fitted to the SLS AMG C197 (Coupé) and R197 (Roadster) from 2010–2015. The standard engine produces 571 hp; the GT produces 591 hp. The engine is handbuilt by AMG with the technician's name engraved on a plaque.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M159 oil starvation issue, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The oil starvation issue is preventable with upgrades. AMG Technical Bulletin TSB-AMG-11-03 addresses this with upgraded dry‑sump scavenge pumps (OEM 159 010 00 01) and baffled sumps. A preventative upgrade costs £1,500–£4,000. If oil starvation has already caused bearing failure, the engine typically requires complete replacement (£8,500–£15,000+). At the first sign of oil pressure loss or knocking, stop driving immediately and book a specialist inspection.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current SLS AMG replacement costs. The GT and Black Series variants typically sit at the higher end of the range. See the Era Map above for generation‑specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an SLS AMG engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The front‑mid engine layout and transaxle configuration increase labour complexity.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: SLS AMG — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential for track use — prevents bearing failure
Essential maintenance — upgrade to ARP bolts
Replace at 60,000 miles — prevents oil contamination
Upgrade to M159 Black Series lifters

rows.problem: Severe oil starvation with bearing failure
rows.whenItMakesSense: Bearing failure typically requires complete engine replacement

buyingChecks.title: Buying This SLS AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes‑AMG service history with regular oil changes (critical for M159 longevity)
* Dry‑sump scavenge pumps upgraded per AMG Technical Bulletin TSB-AMG-11-03
* Camshaft phaser bolts replaced with ARP (or upgraded) units
* No oil leaks around valve covers or camshaft solenoid covers
* No unusual rattling or ticking from the engine
* 2012+ GT model with the 591 hp upgrade
* Black Series (2014) — the ultimate SLS with even more track‑focused engineering
avoidIf: * No service history — the M159 is unforgiving of neglect
* Oil starvation history or bearing replacement — catastrophic failure may be imminent
* Camshaft phaser bolt failure symptoms — budget for engine‑out repair
* Oil leaks around the engine — gaskets may be failing
* Unusual rattling or ticking — valve train or phaser issues likely
* Transmission jerking at low speeds — DCT may need costly attention
* Any history of engine failure or major repairs without documentation

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The SLS AMG is Mercedes‑Benz's modern Gullwing — a handcrafted supercar powered by the M159 6.2‑litre naturally aspirated V8. The engine is a masterpiece, delivering up to 591 hp and 650 Nm torque, with a 7,500 rpm redline and a soundtrack that defines the AMG brand. The M159 features significant improvements over the M156, including dry‑sump lubrication for track use, new camshafts, headers, and improved valve buckets with an anti‑friction coating. However, the SLS demands careful ownership. Common issues include oil starvation during aggressive cornering (addressed by AMG Technical Bulletin TSB-AMG-11-03), camshaft phaser bolt failure (factory bolts are torque‑to‑yield and weak), oil leaks from valve cover gaskets, and crankcase breather valve failure. The 7‑speed DCT can be jerky at low speeds. With typical SLS values ranging from £80,000 to £200,000+, an engine replacement is a major financial commitment — a well‑maintained example with upgraded dry‑sump components, ARP phaser bolts, and full service history is the safest choice. The SLS is a future classic and worth preserving.

cta.label: → Compare SLS AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz SLS AMG (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Dry‑Sump System — Confirm AMG Technical Bulletin TSB-AMG-11-03 upgrades completed (scavenge pumps and baffled sumps) — see the map above
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or ticking (camshaft phaser bolt or valve train warning)
* Oil Leaks — Check around valve covers and camshaft solenoid covers for leaks
* Service History — Including regular oil changes (critical for M159 longevity)
* Camshaft Phaser Bolts — Check if upgraded to ARP or similar high‑strength bolts
* Crankcase Breather — Check for replacement at 60,000 miles
* Transmission — Test DCT at low speeds; any jerkiness may indicate issues
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate phaser, breather or sensor problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
* Gullwing Doors — Check operation and seals; expensive to repair
